question: Why does CUBIC replace Reno's linear cwnd growth with a cubic function of time since the last loss?
options: A. Because linear growth is mathematically incorrect
B. Because CUBIC scales by time rather than by RTT, giving long-RTT flows a chance to compete fairly with short-RTT flows
C. Because the cubic function is easier to compute
explanation: Under Reno, a 100 ms-RTT flow and a 10 ms-RTT flow both add 1 MSS per RTT. In absolute bytes/second the long-RTT flow is 10x slower, so it loses out at any bottleneck shared with a short-RTT flow. CUBIC grows cwnd as a cubic function of wall-clock time since the last loss -- W(t) = C*(t-K)^3 + W_max -- so long-RTT and short-RTT flows advance at the same rate per second (RTT-fairness).

question: How does SACK reduce the bandwidth wasted by a single packet loss compared with cumulative ACK?
options: A. By encrypting the lost segments
B. By allowing the receiver to report up to 3 ranges of bytes received out of order, so the sender retransmits only the missing ranges instead of everything from the first lost byte
C. By increasing the window size
D. By changing the checksum algorithm
explanation: Cumulative ACK tells the sender only 'next byte expected is N', so the sender must retransmit everything from N onward -- potentially many segments. SACK (RFC 2018, refined RFC 2883) lets the receiver list up to 3 ranges of bytes it has buffered out of order. The sender, guided by RFC 3517, retransmits only the gaps. The savings are dramatic on a high-BDP path: a single packet loss costs 35 retransmits under cumulative ACK but only 2 with SACK.

question: Why does BBR achieve better throughput than Reno on a path with deep buffers (bufferbloat)?
options: A. Because BBR runs only on UDP
B. Because BBR is model-based: it paces at the estimated bottleneck bandwidth and targets an inflight of BDP, so it reacts to measured delay growth instead of packet loss
C. Because BBR skips the handshake
D. Because BBR uses a larger initial window
explanation: Reno treats any queue build-up as 'congestion approaching' and halves cwnd on the first loss, which under bufferbloat produces a saw-tooth well below the link capacity. BBR (RFC 9067) estimates two quantities -- BtlBw (max delivery rate over a recent window) and RTprop (min RTT when the path was not queued) -- and tries to hold BDP bytes in flight. It reacts to measured delay growth rather than loss, so a path with 200 ms of queue-induced delay does not force BBR to slow down.
